explanation:
Acute Transverse Myelitis

Acute transverse myelitis (ATM) is a localized inflammatory disorder of the spinal cord that produces motor, sensory, and autonomic dysfunction. It is predominantly a post-infectious or immune-mediated process. The classic presentation involves the acute or subacute onset of back pain, followed by weakness in the lower extremities, sensory loss, and sphincter dysfunction (bowel and bladder incontinence or retention).

Step 1:

When evaluating a patient with sudden onset paraparesis or quadriparesis, the immediate clinical priority is to rule out compressive surgical emergencies (such as spinal epidural abscess, hematoma, or tumor) using a Contrast-enhanced MRI of the spine before diagnosing an intrinsic inflammatory condition like ATM.


Clinical Pearl:

The identification of a distinct sensory level (a dermatomal line below which sensation to pain, temperature, and touch is lost or diminished) is the cardinal physical examination finding that pinpoints the anatomical level of the spinal cord lesion in transverse myelitis. High-yield


Why the correct answer is right

  • Option 4 is the incorrect statement targeted by the question: It is entirely false that "sensation is not affected." Sensation is profoundly affected in transverse myelitis.
  • Patients characteristically develop a sharply defined sensory level corresponding to the involved spinal cord segment. All sensory modalities (pain, temperature, vibration, and proprioception) are typically impaired or absent below this dermatomal level.
  • Dysesthesia and neuropathic pain at or near the level of the lesion are also common early manifestations.

Why the other options are wrong

  • Option 1: This is a true statement. Classic ATM involves the full thickness (or a very large cross-sectional area) of the spinal cord, hence the term "transverse." This distinguishes it from partial myelopathies (like those often seen in multiple sclerosis), which typically involve only a focal portion of the cord.
  • Option 2: This is a true statement. The thoracic spine is the most commonly affected region in pediatric and adult transverse myelitis, which is why paraplegia (rather than quadriplegia) is the most typical motor deficit.
  • Option 3: This is a true statement. On an MRI, the inflamed spinal cord is typically edematous and swollen, creating a central, fusiform (spindle-shaped) enlargement spanning over multiple vertebral segments (usually 3 or more).
  • Option 5: This is a true statement. A large majority of ATM cases are parainfectious, frequently preceded by a recent viral infection (e.g., enterovirus, herpesviruses, Epstein-Barr virus) or an upper respiratory/gastrointestinal illness 1 to 4 weeks prior to neurological symptom onset.

High-Yield Table: Differential Diagnosis of Acute Spinal Cord Syndromes

Clinical and Radiological Differentiation of Acute Myelopathies
Condition Etiology / Pathophysiology Key Clinical Features Diagnostic MRI Findings
Transverse Myelitis Immune-mediated (post-infectious) inflammation Sensory level, paraplegia, autonomic dysfunction (urinary retention). Intrinsic, multi-segmental fusiform swelling; central cord hyperintensity on T2.
Spinal Epidural Abscess Bacterial infection (commonly Staphylococcus aureus) Fever, severe localized back pain, progressive neurological deficit. Extrinsic epidural fluid collection with rim enhancement; cord compression.
Anterior Cord Syndrome Ischemia/infarction (Anterior spinal artery territory) Loss of motor function, pain, and temperature; sparing of proprioception/vibration. "Owl's eye" appearance on axial T2-weighted MRI.
Guillain-Barré Syndrome Post-infectious peripheral nerve demyelination Ascending flaccid paralysis, areflexia; NO sensory level. Normal spinal cord; enhancement of anterior nerve roots may be seen.

Mandatory Notice: Clinical Presentation and Diagnosis of GBS

Overview of Guillain-Barré Syndrome (GBS)

Guillain-Barré Syndrome, specifically Acute Inflammatory Demyelinating Polyradiculoneuropathy (AIDP), is the most common cause of acute flaccid paralysis in healthy children. It is an immune-mediated disorder where the body's immune system attacks the peripheral nerves, usually following a triggering event such as an infection (molecular mimicry). The classic presentation is an ascending paralysis that begins in the lower extremities and progresses cranially (Nelson 22e, Chapter 641, p. 3412).

Step 1: Clinical Recognition

The hallmark of GBS is symmetrical weakness and areflexia (loss of deep tendon reflexes). While sensory symptoms like paresthesia or pain may occur, the motor deficit is usually the most prominent feature. Progression can be rapid, reaching its nadir within 2 to 4 weeks.

Step 2: Diagnostic Evaluation

Diagnosis is primarily clinical, supported by Lumbar Puncture and Electromyography (EMG)/Nerve Conduction Studies (NCS). The classic CSF finding is albuminocytologic dissociation, which refers to a significantly elevated protein level with a normal white blood cell count (Nelson 22e, Chapter 641, p. 3413).

Why the correct answer is right

  • Serology Limitations: While certain antibodies (e.g., anti-GQ1b in Miller Fisher variant or anti-GM1) may be present, serology is NOT the routine or confirmatory test for GBS. The diagnosis is confirmed via clinical criteria (Brighton criteria), CSF findings, and electrophysiologic evidence of demyelination. Serological testing is often negative in the most common forms of pediatric GBS (Nelson 22e, Chapter 641, p. 3414).

Why the other options are wrong

  • A (1): Symmetrical weakness is a defining characteristic of GBS. Asymmetric weakness should prompt a search for alternative diagnoses, such as poliomyelitis or West Nile virus (Nelson 22e, Chapter 641).
  • B (2): Respiratory failure is a major complication. Approximately 20-30% of patients develop respiratory muscle weakness severe enough to require mechanical ventilation. Close monitoring of forced vital capacity (FVC) is essential (Nelson 22e, Chapter 641, p. 3415).
  • D (4): Albuminocytologic dissociation is present in about 80-90% of patients by the end of the second week of illness. It is a highly specific finding that supports the diagnosis of an inflammatory polyneuropathy.
  • E (5): Approximately two-thirds of patients report an antecedent infection 1 to 3 weeks prior to the onset of weakness. Common triggers include Campylobacter jejuni, Cytomegalovirus (CMV), Epstein-Barr virus (EBV), and Mycoplasma pneumoniae.

Clinical Pearl:

The treatment of choice for pediatric GBS is Intravenous Immunoglobulin (IVIG) (2 g/kg over 2–5 days) or Plasmapheresis. Corticosteroids are generally not effective and are not recommended as monotherapy (Nelson 22e, Chapter 641, p. 3415).


[Image of Guillain-Barré Syndrome progression]
Differential Diagnosis of Acute Flaccid Paralysis
Condition Key Differentiating Features Diagnostic Findings
GBS Symmetrical, ascending, areflexic. CSF: High protein, normal cells.
Transverse Myelitis Defined sensory level, bowel/bladder dysfunction early. MRI: Spinal cord lesion.
Tick Paralysis Rapidly ascending; tick often found in hair. Rapid recovery after tick removal.
Botulism Descending paralysis, prominent bulbar signs (ptosis). Stool/serum toxin assay.
Polio Asymmetric weakness, fever/meningeal signs. CSF: Pleocytosis (elevated WBC).
(Ref: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics 22nd Ed; Section on Neuromuscular Disorders)
